articles.headline: Gagangir Terror Attack: Seven Workers Killed in Jammu & Kashmir Tunnel Project
articles.explainer:
• Seven workers involved in a critical tunnel project were killed in a militant attack on their camp in Gagangir, Ganderbal district of Jammu and Kashmir.
• The incident highlights the persistent security challenges in the region, particularly targeting development infrastructure and non-local workers.
• Security forces have launched a massive cordon and search operation to track down the perpetrators.
• The attack underscores the need for enhanced security measures for vital infrastructure projects in sensitive areas.
• Political leaders and civil society have condemned the attack, reiterating calls for peace and stability.
gsSubTopics: Linkages between development and spread of extremism
Role of external state and non-state actors in creating challenges to internal security
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Various security forces and agencies and their mandate
prelimsPoints: Gagangir is located in the Ganderbal district of Jammu and Kashmir.
The attack targeted workers on a key infrastructure project.
Security challenges in J&K often involve external state and non-state actors.
mainsPoints: Analyze the impact of such attacks on development initiatives and investor confidence in J&K.
Discuss the effectiveness of current counter-terrorism strategies in addressing persistent militant threats.
Examine the role of local population and intelligence gathering in preventing such incidents.
Evaluate the challenges in securing critical infrastructure projects in conflict-prone regions.
articles.imageDescription: Security forces conducting search operations in the mountainous terrain of Gagangir, J&K.
articles.trivia: The Zojila Tunnel, a strategically important project in J&K, aims to provide all-weather connectivity between Srinagar, Kargil, and Leh.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Internal Security
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Linkages between development and spread of extremism

articles.headline: India Hosts Inaugural India-Africa Defence Dialogue, Enhancing Strategic Ties
articles.explainer:
• India hosted the first-ever India-Africa Defence Dialogue, bringing together defence ministers and high-ranking officials from African nations.
• The dialogue focused on strengthening defence cooperation, capacity building, and joint efforts in maritime security and counter-terrorism.
• Discussions included transfer of defence technology, joint exercises, and training programs for African armed forces.
• This initiative is part of India's broader engagement with the African continent, aligning with its 'SAGAR' (Security and Growth for All in the Region) vision.
• The dialogue aims to foster a secure and stable Indo-Pacific region through collaborative defence partnerships.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: The 'SAGAR' vision stands for 'Security and Growth for All in the Region'.
India has been increasing its defence exports and cooperation with African nations.
The dialogue aims to enhance collaboration in maritime domain awareness and anti-piracy operations.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's strategic rationale behind deepening defence ties with African nations.
Analyze the potential benefits and challenges of defence technology transfer to African countries.
Evaluate how India's 'SAGAR' vision contributes to regional security and stability in the Indo-Pacific.
Examine the role of defence diplomacy in strengthening India's geopolitical influence in the Global South.
articles.imageDescription: Representatives from India and African nations at the Defence Dialogue.
articles.trivia: India is one of the largest contributors of troops to UN peacekeeping missions in Africa.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Strict Interpretation of Forest Conservation Act, Emphasizes Deemed Forests
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court delivered a landmark ruling, reinforcing the strict application of the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980, especially concerning 'deemed forests'.
• The judgment clarifies that areas recorded as forests in government records, irrespective of their legal classification, fall under the Act's purview.
• This decision significantly impacts infrastructure projects and development activities requiring forest land diversion, mandating prior central government approval.
• It also has implications for tribal rights and the implementation of the Forest Rights Act, 2006, by protecting traditional forest-dwelling communities.
• The Court directed states to expedite the identification and demarcation of 'deemed forests' as per the T.N. Godavarman Thirumulpad case.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
prelimsPoints: The Forest (Conservation) Act was enacted in 1980.
The concept of 'deemed forests' was introduced by the Supreme Court in the T.N. Godavarman Thirumulpad case (1996).
Prior central government approval is mandatory for diverting forest land for non-forest purposes.
The Forest Rights Act, 2006, grants rights to forest-dwelling Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers.
mainsPoints: Analyze the implications of the Supreme Court's ruling on the balance between development and environmental conservation.
Discuss the challenges in identifying and demarcating 'deemed forests' across different states.
Examine how this judgment strengthens the protection of biodiversity and the rights of indigenous communities.
Evaluate the role of the judiciary in environmental governance and policy implementation in India.
articles.imageDescription: Lush green forest canopy, symbolizing forest conservation efforts.
articles.trivia: The T.N. Godavarman Thirumulpad case is often referred to as the 'forest case' and has significantly shaped forest jurisprudence in India.

articles.headline: India Champions Global Adoption of Digital Public Infrastructure Model
articles.explainer:
• India is actively promoting its successful Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) model, comprising Aadhaar, UPI, and ONDC, for global adoption, especially in the Global South.
• The initiative aims to foster financial inclusion, streamline public services, and boost digital economies in developing nations.
• Discussions are ongoing at various international forums, including the G20, on interoperability, data governance, and technology transfer.
• India's DPI model offers a cost-effective and scalable solution for digital transformation, reducing reliance on proprietary systems.
• The push highlights India's growing influence in digital diplomacy and its commitment to shared prosperity.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
prelimsPoints: Aadhaar is India's unique identification system.
UPI (Unified Payments Interface) is a real-time payment system.
ONDC (Open Network for Digital Commerce) is an initiative to democratize e-commerce.
India's DPI model is often referred to as the 'India Stack'.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key components of India's Digital Public Infrastructure and their impact on socio-economic development.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities for global adoption of the DPI model, particularly in developing countries.
Examine how India's digital diplomacy, through DPI, can enhance its leadership in the Global South.
Evaluate the potential risks associated with data privacy and security in a widely adopted DPI framework.
articles.imageDescription: Infographic showing the interconnectedness of Aadhaar, UPI, and ONDC as part of India's DPI.
articles.trivia: UPI transactions surpassed 10 billion per month in 2023, showcasing its widespread adoption.

articles.headline: ISRO Provides Update on Shukrayaan-1, India's Upcoming Venus Orbiter Mission
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) has provided an update on the progress of Shukrayaan-1, India's ambitious mission to Venus.
• The mission aims to study Venus's atmospheric chemistry, surface composition, and subsurface structure using advanced scientific instruments.
• Shukrayaan-1 is slated for launch by late 2024 or early 2025, marking India's first dedicated mission to Venus.
• The mission involves collaboration with international partners for some payloads, enhancing global scientific cooperation.
• This venture underscores India's growing capabilities in planetary exploration and its contribution to space science.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Awareness in the fields of Space
prelimsPoints: Shukrayaan-1 is India's proposed mission to Venus.
Venus is often called Earth's 'sister planet' due to similar size and mass.
ISRO has previously launched successful missions to Mars (Mangalyaan) and the Moon (Chandrayaan series).
The mission will carry a Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) to map Venus's surface.
mainsPoints: Discuss the scientific objectives and potential discoveries of the Shukrayaan-1 mission.
Analyze the significance of India's planetary exploration program for its scientific and technological advancement.
Examine the role of international collaboration in complex space missions like Shukrayaan-1.
Evaluate the broader socio-economic benefits derived from advancements in space technology.
articles.imageDescription: Artistic rendition of the Shukrayaan-1 orbiter orbiting Venus.
articles.trivia: Venus is the hottest planet in our solar system, with surface temperatures hot enough to melt lead, despite being farther from the Sun than Mercury.

articles.headline: PM-Vishwakarma Scheme Shows Promising Results in Empowering Artisans and Craftsmen
articles.explainer:
• A recent review of the PM-Vishwakarma Scheme indicates positive initial outcomes in uplifting traditional artisans and craftsmen.
• The scheme provides skill development training, access to modern tools, collateral-free credit, and market linkages to 'Vishwakarma' communities.
• It aims to preserve India's rich heritage of traditional crafts while integrating artisans into the formal economy.
• Early successes include increased income generation and improved access to finance for many beneficiaries.
• Challenges remain in ensuring wider outreach, digital literacy, and sustainable market access for all traditional crafts.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it
prelimsPoints: The PM-Vishwakarma Scheme was launched in 2023.
It targets 18 traditional trades, including carpenters, goldsmiths, potters, barbers, etc.
Key components include skill training, toolkit incentives, collateral-free credit, and marketing support.
The scheme is a central sector scheme, fully funded by the Union Government.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the PM-Vishwakarma Scheme in preserving traditional crafts and empowering informal sector workers.
Discuss the challenges faced by traditional artisans in India and how government schemes address them.
Evaluate the role of skill development and market linkages in fostering inclusive growth in the MSME sector.
Suggest measures to improve the outreach and effectiveness of welfare schemes for vulnerable communities.
articles.imageDescription: An Indian artisan meticulously working on a traditional craft.
articles.trivia: India's handicraft sector is one of the largest employment generators after agriculture, preserving diverse cultural heritage.

articles.headline: National Green Hydrogen Mission Drives Investment in Renewable Energy Sector
articles.explainer:
• India's National Green Hydrogen Mission is successfully catalyzing significant domestic and international investments in the renewable energy sector.
• The mission focuses on developing green hydrogen production capacity and promoting its use in hard-to-abate sectors like fertilizers, refining, and steel.
• Incentives under the Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition (SIGHT) Programme are boosting electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production.
• This initiative is crucial for India to achieve its decarbonization targets, enhance energy security, and become a global hub for green hydrogen.
• Challenges include the high initial cost of green hydrogen production and the need for robust infrastructure development.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life; Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
articles.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy, Business Standard
prelimsPoints: Green hydrogen is produced by splitting water using renewable electricity (electrolysis).
The National Green Hydrogen Mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export.
The SIGHT Programme provides financial incentives for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production.
India has set a target to achieve Net-Zero emissions by 2070.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of the National Green Hydrogen Mission for India's energy security and climate goals.
Discuss the economic potential of green hydrogen for various industries and its role in decarbonization.
Examine the challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production and adoption in India.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government incentives like the SIGHT Programme in driving investment and innovation.
articles.imageDescription: A conceptual image showing a green hydrogen production plant with wind turbines and solar panels.
articles.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, but it rarely exists freely on Earth.

articles.headline: India Continues to Champion Global South Agenda Post-G20 Presidency
articles.explainer:
• India maintains its active role in advocating for the interests of the Global South, even after concluding its G20 presidency.
• The focus remains on key issues such as equitable climate finance, debt relief for developing nations, and technology transfer.
• India is actively engaging with various multilateral platforms and regional groupings to amplify the 'Voice of the Global South'.
• This ongoing diplomatic push aims to ensure that the concerns of developing countries are integrated into global decision-making.
• Initiatives like the 'Voice of Global South Summit' continue to serve as a platform for these discussions.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
articles.source: Ministry of External Affairs, The Hindu
prelimsPoints: India held the G20 Presidency from December 1, 2022, to November 30, 2023.
The 'Voice of Global South Summit' was initiated by India.
The concept of 'Common But Differentiated Responsibilities' (CBDR) is central to climate finance discussions.
The African Union was admitted as a permanent member of the G20 during India's presidency.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's leadership role in representing the interests of the Global South on international platforms.
Discuss the key challenges faced by developing countries that India aims to address through its diplomatic efforts.
Evaluate the impact of India's G20 presidency on shaping global agendas and fostering South-South cooperation.
Examine the concept of 'climate justice' and its relevance in current international climate negotiations.
articles.imageDescription: A world map highlighting countries of the Global South, symbolizing India's outreach.
articles.trivia: The term 'Global South' broadly refers to countries in Asia, Africa, and Latin America, often characterized by shared experiences of colonialism and economic development challenges.

articles.headline: Government Launches New Initiative for Revitalization of Traditional Water Bodies
articles.explainer:
• The Union Government has unveiled a new national initiative aimed at revitalizing and conserving traditional water bodies across the country.
• The program focuses on restoring ancient stepwells, ponds, lakes, and other indigenous water harvesting structures.
• Objectives include enhancing local water security, recharging groundwater, conserving aquatic biodiversity, and promoting cultural heritage.
• The initiative emphasizes community participation and the integration of traditional knowledge with modern conservation techniques.
• It seeks to build resilience against climate change impacts and address water scarcity challenges at the local level.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Water resources
articles.source: Ministry of Jal Shakti, Down To Earth
prelimsPoints: Traditional water harvesting structures include stepwells (baolis), johads, tanks, and lakes.
The Ministry of Jal Shakti is responsible for water resource management.
India faces significant challenges related to groundwater depletion.
Jal Jeevan Mission and Atal Bhujal Yojana are other major water-related government schemes.
mainsPoints: Discuss the historical significance and ecological importance of traditional water bodies in India.
Analyze the role of community participation and traditional knowledge in successful water conservation efforts.
Evaluate how the revitalization of these structures contributes to climate change adaptation and water security.
Examine the challenges in implementing such initiatives across diverse geographical and socio-economic contexts.
articles.imageDescription: A beautifully restored ancient stepwell (baoli) in Rajasthan.
articles.trivia: Many traditional water bodies are architectural marvels, serving not only as water sources but also as social and cultural hubs.

articles.headline: Private Sector Participation Bolsters India's Defence Indigenization Efforts
articles.explainer:
• Government policies promoting 'Aatmanirbhar Bharat' (self-reliant India) in defence are increasingly involving the private sector.
• Increased participation from Indian private companies in defence manufacturing, R&D, and technology development is observed.
• Initiatives like defence corridors, positive indigenization lists, and simplified procurement procedures are driving this change.
• The objective is to reduce import dependency, foster innovation, and position India as a defence exporter.
• Challenges include bridging technology gaps, ensuring quality control, and streamlining regulatory processes for smaller players.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth
prelimsPoints: The 'Aatmanirbhar Bharat' initiative aims for self-reliance across various sectors, including defence.
Defence corridors have been established in Uttar Pradesh and Tamil Nadu.
The Positive Indigenization List restricts the import of certain defence items, encouraging domestic production.
DRDO (Defence Research and Development Organisation) is the premier R&D agency for defence.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of private sector involvement in achieving defence indigenization and self-reliance for India.
Discuss the policy measures undertaken by the government to boost domestic defence manufacturing.
Examine the economic and strategic benefits of reducing defence import dependency.
Identify the challenges that persist in making India a global defence manufacturing and export hub.
articles.imageDescription: An Indian-made fighter jet being assembled in a private defence manufacturing facility.
articles.trivia: India is among the top global importers of defence equipment, but its indigenization efforts are rapidly changing this trend.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Economy
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Make in India and Defence Manufacturing

articles.headline: Government Explores Ethical Framework for AI Integration in Public Services
articles.explainer:
• The Indian government is actively developing a comprehensive ethical framework for the responsible deployment of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in public services.
• The focus is on ensuring fairness, transparency, accountability, and data privacy in AI applications across sectors like healthcare, education, and justice.
• NITI Aayog and the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) are leading consultations with stakeholders.
• The framework aims to balance technological innovation with societal impact, addressing concerns like algorithmic bias and job displacement.
• India seeks to position itself as a leader in developing ethical AI solutions for the public good.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
E-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential; citizens charters, transparency & accountability and institutional and other measures
prelimsPoints: NITI Aayog released 'National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence' in 2018.
MeitY is the nodal ministry for IT, electronics, and internet policy.
AI applications in governance include predictive policing, healthcare diagnostics, and personalized education.
Concerns like 'algorithmic bias' refer to systematic and unfair discrimination by AI systems.
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential benefits and challenges of integrating Artificial Intelligence into public services in India.
Analyze the key ethical considerations that must be addressed in developing an AI governance framework.
Evaluate the role of NITI Aayog and MeitY in shaping India's AI strategy and policy.
Examine how an ethical AI framework can ensure equitable access and prevent discrimination in public service delivery.
articles.imageDescription: Abstract representation of AI neural networks interacting with government services.
articles.trivia: India launched the 'Responsible AI for Youth' program to provide foundational knowledge and skills in AI to young students.

articles.headline: Delayed Monsoon Withdrawal Impacts Rabi Sowing Preparations, Concerns for Farmers
articles.explainer:
• The delayed withdrawal of the Southwest Monsoon in several parts of India is raising concerns for the upcoming Rabi crop season.
• Prolonged rainfall has impacted soil moisture conditions and delayed field preparation for sowing winter crops.
• Farmers in some regions are facing challenges due to waterlogging, while others worry about insufficient moisture for Rabi if the withdrawal is abrupt.
• The government and agricultural experts are monitoring the situation, with contingency plans being discussed to support affected farmers.
• This situation could potentially impact agricultural output and contribute to food inflation if not managed effectively.
gsSubTopics: Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes

Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers
articles.source: IMD, Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare
prelimsPoints: Rabi crops are winter crops, typically sown from October to December and harvested from April to June (e.g., wheat, barley, mustard).
Kharif crops are monsoon crops, sown from June to July and harvested from September to October (e.g., rice, maize, jowar).
The Indian Meteorological Department (IMD) monitors monsoon patterns.
Delayed monsoon withdrawal can lead to increased pest attacks and diseases in standing crops.
mainsPoints: Analyze the impact of changing monsoon patterns on India's agricultural sector and food security.
Discuss the challenges faced by farmers due to erratic weather events and the effectiveness of government support mechanisms.
Examine the interrelationship between monsoon performance, agricultural output, and food inflation.
Suggest sustainable agricultural practices and policy interventions to enhance farmers' resilience to climate variability.
articles.imageDescription: A farmer inspecting his fields after heavy rainfall, showing concern for Rabi sowing.
articles.trivia: India's economy is heavily dependent on the monsoon, with over 50% of its agricultural land still rain-fed.

articles.headline: India-EU Trade and Technology Council Focuses on Critical Minerals and Green Technologies
articles.explainer:
• The second meeting of the India-EU Trade and Technology Council (TTC) saw significant discussions on critical minerals and green technologies.
• Both sides emphasized securing resilient supply chains for critical raw materials essential for the digital and green transitions.
• Cooperation on renewable energy, sustainable manufacturing, and circular economy principles was a key agenda item.
• The TTC aims to deepen strategic cooperation, facilitate trade, and address technological challenges between India and the European Union.
• This partnership is crucial for diversifying supply chains and strengthening strategic autonomy in a changing geopolitical landscape.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
articles.source: Ministry of Commerce & Industry, European Commission
prelimsPoints: The India-EU Trade and Technology Council was established in 2022.
Critical minerals like Lithium, Cobalt, and Rare Earth Elements are vital for electric vehicles and renewable energy technologies.
The EU is one of India's largest trading partners.
The TTC is a strategic mechanism to manage trade, technology, and security issues.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of the India-EU Trade and Technology Council for both partners in the current geopolitical scenario.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities in securing critical mineral supply chains for India's energy transition.
Examine how cooperation in green technologies can foster sustainable development and economic growth.
Evaluate the potential of the TTC to diversify trade and reduce dependencies on single-source suppliers.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and the European Union displayed together, symbolizing cooperation.
articles.trivia: The EU is India's third-largest trading partner, accounting for 10.8% of India's total trade in 2022-23.

articles.headline: NEP 2020 Implementation Sees Progress in Higher Education Reforms
articles.explainer:
• The implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 is showing discernible progress, particularly in reforming higher education.
• Key reforms include promoting a multidisciplinary approach, flexible curriculum, Academic Bank of Credits (ABC), and vocational education integration.
• Universities and colleges are gradually adopting the new credit system and offering multiple entry/exit options for students.
• The focus is on making higher education more holistic, accessible, and aligned with industry needs.
• Challenges persist in faculty recruitment, infrastructure development, and ensuring equitable implementation across diverse institutions.
gsSubTopics: Development processes and the development industry—the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 replaced the National Policy on Education of 1986.
The Academic Bank of Credits (ABC) allows students to store and transfer academic credits.
The policy aims to increase the Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in higher education to 50% by 2035.
Multidisciplinary approach means integrating various subjects like arts, science, commerce, and vocational studies.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of NEP 2020 for India's higher education system.
Discuss the key reforms introduced in higher education under NEP 2020 and their expected outcomes.
Examine the challenges in implementing NEP 2020, particularly concerning faculty, infrastructure, and institutional readiness.
Evaluate how the policy addresses the needs of India's demographic dividend and future workforce requirements.
articles.imageDescription: Students engaging in a multidisciplinary learning environment at a university campus.
articles.trivia: NEP 2020 is the first education policy of the 21st century in India.

articles.headline: Smart Cities Mission Progress Reviewed, Focus on Sustainable Urban Development
articles.explainer:
• A recent review assessed the progress of the Smart Cities Mission, highlighting achievements and areas for improvement.
• The mission focuses on integrated urban planning, digital infrastructure, and citizen-centric services to enhance livability.
• Key successes include improvements in urban mobility, waste management, and the establishment of Integrated Command and Control Centres (ICCCs).
• The review emphasized the need for greater capacity building for urban local bodies and ensuring financial sustainability of projects.
• Future efforts will prioritize climate-resilient urban infrastructure and equitable access to smart solutions.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
articles.source: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
prelimsPoints: The Smart Cities Mission was launched in 2015.
It aims to promote cities that provide core infrastructure, a clean and sustainable environment, and a decent quality of life.
Integrated Command and Control Centres (ICCCs) are central to smart governance and monitoring.
Other related urban missions include AMRUT (Atal Mission for Rejuvenation and Urban Transformation) and HRIDAY (Heritage City Development and Augmentation Yojana).
mainsPoints: Analyze the objectives and achievements of the Smart Cities Mission in transforming India's urban landscape.
Discuss the challenges faced in implementing smart city projects, including funding, governance, and citizen participation.
Examine how the mission contributes to sustainable urban development and climate resilience.
Suggest measures to enhance the effectiveness and inclusivity of smart city initiatives for all urban residents.
articles.imageDescription: A futuristic cityscape with smart infrastructure and green spaces, representing a smart city.
articles.trivia: India is projected to have 600 million people living in urban areas by 2030, making urban planning crucial.

articles.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Policy Rates Amidst Persistent Inflationary Concerns
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) decided to maintain the repo rate and other policy rates at current levels.
• The decision comes amidst persistent concerns about elevated retail inflation, particularly food inflation, and global economic uncertainties.
• The RBI reiterated its commitment to aligning inflation with the target while supporting economic growth.
• The central bank also highlighted strong domestic demand and robust credit growth as positive indicators for the Indian economy.
• Future policy decisions will remain data-dependent, with a close watch on inflation trajectory and global developments.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is a six-member body responsible for setting the benchmark interest rate (repo rate).
The primary objective of monetary policy in India is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
Retail inflation is measured by the Consumer Price Index (CPI).
mainsPoints: Analyze the role of the RBI's Monetary Policy Committee in managing inflation and fostering economic growth in India.
Discuss the factors contributing to persistent inflationary pressures in the Indian economy, particularly food inflation.
Examine the impact of a 'status quo' monetary policy on various sectors of the economy, including lending and investment.
Evaluate the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control with the need to support economic recovery and growth.
articles.imageDescription: The Reserve Bank of India headquarters building in Mumbai.
articles.trivia: The RBI was established on April 1, 1935, in accordance with the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

articles.headline: Border Roads Organisation Accelerates Infrastructure Development in Critical Border Areas
articles.explainer:
• The Border Roads Organisation (BRO) is significantly accelerating infrastructure development projects in India's critical border regions.
• Focus areas include constructing all-weather roads, bridges, and tunnels to enhance connectivity and troop mobility.
• These projects are strategically important for national security, facilitating rapid deployment and logistical support for armed forces.
• The use of advanced construction techniques and technologies is improving project execution speed and quality.
• Challenges include harsh climatic conditions, difficult terrain, and the need for environmental clearances.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Role of various security forces and agencies and their mandate
prelimsPoints: The Border Roads Organisation (BRO) works under the Ministry of Defence.
BRO is responsible for constructing and maintaining road networks in India's border areas.
Strategic tunnels like the Atal Tunnel and Sela Tunnel improve all-weather connectivity.
The Line of Actual Control (LAC) is the de facto border between India and China.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic significance of robust border infrastructure for India's national security and defence preparedness.
Discuss the challenges faced by the Border Roads Organisation in executing projects in difficult terrains and harsh climatic conditions.
Examine the socio-economic benefits of improved connectivity for border populations.
Evaluate the role of infrastructure development as a deterrent and a facilitator of military operations in border areas.
articles.imageDescription: BRO workers constructing a road in a high-altitude, snow-covered border region.
articles.trivia: BRO was formed in 1960 and has built over 60,000 km of roads and over 50,000 meters of bridges in difficult terrains.

articles.headline: Conservation Efforts Intensify for Critically Endangered Great Indian Bustard
articles.explainer:
• Renewed and intensified conservation efforts are underway to protect the critically endangered Great Indian Bustard (GIB).
• The initiatives include habitat restoration, captive breeding programs, and measures to mitigate threats from power lines and agricultural expansion.
• Collaboration between central and state governments, along with wildlife organizations, is crucial for the GIB's survival.
• The GIB population has dwindled drastically, making it one of India's most endangered bird species.
• Public awareness campaigns are also being launched to garner support for its protection.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Biodiversity
Protected Areas
articles.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change, WWF India
prelimsPoints: The Great Indian Bustard (GIB) is listed as 'Critically Endangered' by the IUCN Red List.
Its primary habitats are grasslands and scrublands in Rajasthan, Gujarat, Maharashtra, and Karnataka.
Major threats include power lines, habitat loss, and hunting.
The GIB is protected under Schedule I of the Wildlife Protection Act, 1972.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to the critically endangered status of the Great Indian Bustard.
Discuss the various conservation strategies being implemented for the GIB and their effectiveness.
Examine the challenges in balancing development projects with wildlife conservation, particularly for grassland species.
Evaluate the importance of community involvement and inter-state cooperation in successful species recovery programs.
articles.imageDescription: A majestic Great Indian Bustard walking in its natural grassland habitat.
articles.trivia: The Great Indian Bustard is the state bird of Rajasthan.

articles.headline: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission Expands Reach, Focuses on Universal Health Coverage
articles.explainer:
• The Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) is significantly expanding its reach to create a seamless online platform for health services.
• The mission aims to develop the backbone necessary to support the integrated digital health infrastructure of the country.
• Key components include the Ayushman Bharat Health Account (ABHA) number, healthcare professional registry, and health facility registry.
• The expansion focuses on enabling universal health coverage and improving access to healthcare services through digital means.
• Challenges include digital literacy, data privacy concerns, and integrating diverse healthcare providers.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
E-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential
articles.source: National Health Authority (NHA), Ministry of Health & Family Welfare
prelimsPoints: ABDM was launched nationwide in September 2021.
The ABHA (Ayushman Bharat Health Account) number is a unique 14-digit health ID.
The National Health Authority (NHA) is the implementing agency for ABDM.
Ayushman Bharat also includes PM-JAY (Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana) for health insurance.
mainsPoints: Analyze the potential of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission to transform healthcare delivery and achieve universal health coverage in India.
Discuss the key features and benefits of creating an integrated digital health infrastructure.
Examine the challenges related to data privacy, digital literacy, and interoperability in implementing ABDM.
Evaluate how digital health initiatives can address disparities in healthcare access and quality across different regions.
articles.imageDescription: A smartphone screen displaying the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission logo and health services.
articles.trivia: India's digital health ecosystem aims to connect patients, doctors, and other healthcare stakeholders digitally.

highlights: Seven workers killed in militant attack on tunnel project camp in J&K's Gagangir, raising security concerns.
India hosts inaugural India-Africa Defence Dialogue, focusing on strategic cooperation and maritime security.
Supreme Court upholds strict interpretation of Forest Conservation Act, emphasizing protection of 'deemed forests'.
India champions global adoption of its Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) model for financial inclusion.
National Green Hydrogen Mission drives significant investment, aiming for decarbonization and energy security.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the Great Indian Bustard (GIB), consider the following statements:
1. It is listed as 'Critically Endangered' by the IUCN Red List.
2. Its primary habitats are found exclusively in the Thar Desert of Rajasthan.
3. It is protected under Schedule I of the Wildlife Protection Act, 1972.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: The Great Indian Bustard (GIB) is indeed listed as 'Critically Endangered' by the IUCN Red List. Statement 2 is incorrect: While the Thar Desert in Rajasthan is a significant habitat, GIBs are also found in parts of Gujarat, Maharashtra, and Karnataka, not exclusively in Thar. Statement 3 is correct: The GIB is provided the highest level of protection under Schedule I of the Wildlife Protection Act, 1972.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following components are part of India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) model, often referred to as 'India Stack'?
1. Aadhaar
2. Unified Payments Interface (UPI)
3. Open Network for Digital Commerce (ONDC)
4. Goods and Services Tax Network (GSTN)
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Aadhaar, UPI, and ONDC are considered core components of India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) or 'India Stack', facilitating identity, payments, and commerce respectively. GSTN (Goods and Services Tax Network) is an important digital infrastructure for taxation but is generally not grouped with Aadhaar, UPI, and ONDC as part of the core 'India Stack' for broader public service delivery and financial inclusion, though it is a significant digital initiative.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the National Green Hydrogen Mission:
1. Its primary objective is to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export.
2. The Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition (SIGHT) Programme is a component of this mission.
3. Green hydrogen is produced by the electrolysis of water using renewable energy.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: All three statements are correct. The National Green Hydrogen Mission aims to position India as a leader in green hydrogen production and export. The SIGHT Programme is indeed a key component providing financial incentives. Green hydrogen is defined by its production method: electrolysis of water powered by renewable electricity.

prelimsMocks.question: The term 'deemed forests' often appears in the context of environmental protection in India. Which of the following best describes 'deemed forests' as per Supreme Court rulings?
options: (a) Forest areas officially notified under the Indian Forest Act, 1927.
(b) Any land recorded as 'forest' in government records, irrespective of ownership or classification.
(c) Reserved forests and protected forests as defined by the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980.
(d) Forest land owned by private individuals, requiring compensation for acquisition.
prelimsMocks.answer: (b) Any land recorded as 'forest' in government records, irrespective of ownership or classification.
prelimsMocks.explanation: As per the Supreme Court's landmark judgment in the T.N. Godavarman Thirumulpad case (1996), 'deemed forests' refer to any area that satisfies the dictionary meaning of a forest or has been recorded as forest in any government record, irrespective of its legal classification or ownership. This broad definition ensures wider protection under the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements correctly describe the objectives of the PM-Vishwakarma Scheme?
1. To provide skill training and credit support to traditional artisans and craftsmen.
2. To promote the export of Indian handicrafts to international markets.
3. To preserve India's rich heritage of traditional crafts.
4. To integrate 'Vishwakarma' communities into the formal economy.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statements 1, 3, and 4 correctly describe the objectives of the PM-Vishwakarma Scheme. The scheme focuses on skill development, providing credit, preserving traditional crafts, and integrating artisans into the formal economy. While promoting exports might be an indirect benefit, it is not a primary, direct objective of the scheme as outlined. The scheme's core is domestic empowerment and preservation.

mainsMocks.question: The recent militant attack in Gagangir, J&K, highlights the complex interplay between security challenges and infrastructure development in conflict-prone regions. Discuss the multi-pronged strategy required to ensure security for development projects while addressing the root causes of extremism in Jammu & Kashmir.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism; Linkages between development and spread of extremism.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the context of the Gagangir attack. Discuss the dual challenge of protecting development and counter-extremism. Outline a multi-pronged strategy: enhanced physical security, intelligence gathering, community engagement, economic development, counter-radicalization, and cross-border management. Conclude with the need for a holistic and sustained approach.

mainsMocks.question: India's growing role as a champion of the Global South is evident in its post-G20 presidency diplomatic engagements. Analyze the key issues that India champions for the Global South and evaluate the challenges and opportunities in strengthening South-South cooperation in the current geopolitical order.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests; Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
mainsMocks.approach: Define India's role as a voice for the Global South and refer to G20 presidency. Identify key issues championed: climate finance, debt relief, technology transfer, food and energy security, UN reforms. Discuss opportunities (collective bargaining power, shared development experiences, alternative development models) and challenges (internal divisions, resource constraints, geopolitical pressures from developed nations). Conclude on India's potential to shape a more equitable global order.

mainsMocks.question: The Supreme Court's ruling on the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980, and 'deemed forests' underscores the judiciary's role in environmental governance. Discuss the implications of this judgment for balancing development with environmental protection and tribal rights in India.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment; GS2: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation; Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the SC ruling and the concept of 'deemed forests'. Elaborate on its implications: stricter environmental clearances for projects, protection of unclassified forests, impact on infrastructure. Discuss the balance: how it restricts unbridled development but safeguards critical ecosystems and the rights of forest-dwelling communities (FRA 2006). Conclude on the judiciary's role in upholding constitutional environmental principles and guiding sustainable development.

quickBytes.text: The Border Roads Organisation (BRO) operates under the Ministry of Defence.
The 'SAGAR' vision (Security and Growth for All in the Region) is a strategic vision of India for maritime cooperation.
The 'deemed forests' concept was introduced by the Supreme Court in the T.N. Godavarman Thirumulpad case (1996).
The Academic Bank of Credits (ABC) allows students in higher education to store and transfer academic credits.
Shukrayaan-1 is India's upcoming planetary mission to Venus.
The National Green Hydrogen Mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen.
The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is responsible for setting the benchmark interest rate (repo rate).
The Great Indian Bustard (GIB) is listed as 'Critically Endangered' by IUCN.
The Ayushman Bharat Health Account (ABHA) is a unique 14-digit health ID under ABDM.
Rabi crops are winter crops, typically sown from October to December.

GS1.headline: Government Launches New Initiative for Revitalization of Traditional Water Bodies
GS1.explainer:
• The Union Government has unveiled a new national initiative aimed at revitalizing and conserving traditional water bodies across the country.
• The program focuses on restoring ancient stepwells, ponds, lakes, and other indigenous water harvesting structures.
• Objectives include enhancing local water security, recharging groundwater, conserving aquatic biodiversity, and promoting cultural heritage.
• The initiative emphasizes community participation and the integration of traditional knowledge with modern conservation techniques.
• It seeks to build resilience against climate change impacts and address water scarcity challenges at the local level.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Water resources
GS1.source: Ministry of Jal Shakti, Down To Earth
prelimsPoints: Traditional water harvesting structures include stepwells (baolis), johads, tanks, and lakes.
The Ministry of Jal Shakti is responsible for water resource management.
India faces significant challenges related to groundwater depletion.
Jal Jeevan Mission and Atal Bhujal Yojana are other major water-related government schemes.
mainsPoints: Discuss the historical significance and ecological importance of traditional water bodies in India.
Analyze the role of community participation and traditional knowledge in successful water conservation efforts.
Evaluate how the revitalization of these structures contributes to climate change adaptation and water security.
Examine the challenges in implementing such initiatives across diverse geographical and socio-economic contexts.
GS1.imageDescription: A beautifully restored ancient stepwell (baoli) in Rajasthan.
GS1.trivia: Many traditional water bodies are architectural marvels, serving not only as water sources but also as social and cultural hubs.

GS1.headline: Delayed Monsoon Withdrawal Impacts Rabi Sowing Preparations, Concerns for Farmers
GS1.explainer:
• The delayed withdrawal of the Southwest Monsoon in several parts of India is raising concerns for the upcoming Rabi crop season.
• Prolonged rainfall has impacted soil moisture conditions and delayed field preparation for sowing winter crops.
• Farmers in some regions are facing challenges due to waterlogging, while others worry about insufficient moisture for Rabi if the withdrawal is abrupt.
• The government and agricultural experts are monitoring the situation, with contingency plans being discussed to support affected farmers.
• This situation could potentially impact agricultural output and contribute to food inflation if not managed effectively.
gsSubTopics: Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes

Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers
GS1.source: IMD, Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare
prelimsPoints: Rabi crops are winter crops, typically sown from October to December and harvested from April to June (e.g., wheat, barley, mustard).
Kharif crops are monsoon crops, sown from June to July and harvested from September to October (e.g., rice, maize, jowar).
The Indian Meteorological Department (IMD) monitors monsoon patterns.
Delayed monsoon withdrawal can lead to increased pest attacks and diseases in standing crops.
mainsPoints: Analyze the impact of changing monsoon patterns on India's agricultural sector and food security.
Discuss the challenges faced by farmers due to erratic weather events and the effectiveness of government support mechanisms.
Examine the interrelationship between monsoon performance, agricultural output, and food inflation.
Suggest sustainable agricultural practices and policy interventions to enhance farmers' resilience to climate variability.
GS1.imageDescription: A farmer inspecting his fields after heavy rainfall, showing concern for Rabi sowing.
GS1.trivia: India's economy is heavily dependent on the monsoon, with over 50% of its agricultural land still rain-fed.

GS2.headline: India Hosts Inaugural India-Africa Defence Dialogue, Enhancing Strategic Ties
GS2.explainer:
• India hosted the first-ever India-Africa Defence Dialogue, bringing together defence ministers and high-ranking officials from African nations.
• The dialogue focused on strengthening defence cooperation, capacity building, and joint efforts in maritime security and counter-terrorism.
• Discussions included transfer of defence technology, joint exercises, and training programs for African armed forces.
• This initiative is part of India's broader engagement with the African continent, aligning with its 'SAGAR' (Security and Growth for All in the Region) vision.
• The dialogue aims to foster a secure and stable Indo-Pacific region through collaborative defence partnerships.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: The 'SAGAR' vision stands for 'Security and Growth for All in the Region'.
India has been increasing its defence exports and cooperation with African nations.
The dialogue aims to enhance collaboration in maritime domain awareness and anti-piracy operations.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's strategic rationale behind deepening defence ties with African nations.
Analyze the potential benefits and challenges of defence technology transfer to African countries.
Evaluate how India's 'SAGAR' vision contributes to regional security and stability in the Indo-Pacific.
Examine the role of defence diplomacy in strengthening India's geopolitical influence in the Global South.
GS2.imageDescription: Representatives from India and African nations at the Defence Dialogue.
GS2.trivia: India is one of the largest contributors of troops to UN peacekeeping missions in Africa.

GS2.headline: PM-Vishwakarma Scheme Shows Promising Results in Empowering Artisans and Craftsmen
GS2.explainer:
• A recent review of the PM-Vishwakarma Scheme indicates positive initial outcomes in uplifting traditional artisans and craftsmen.
• The scheme provides skill development training, access to modern tools, collateral-free credit, and market linkages to 'Vishwakarma' communities.
• It aims to preserve India's rich heritage of traditional crafts while integrating artisans into the formal economy.
• Early successes include increased income generation and improved access to finance for many beneficiaries.
• Challenges remain in ensuring wider outreach, digital literacy, and sustainable market access for all traditional crafts.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it
prelimsPoints: The PM-Vishwakarma Scheme was launched in 2023.
It targets 18 traditional trades, including carpenters, goldsmiths, potters, barbers, etc.
Key components include skill training, toolkit incentives, collateral-free credit, and marketing support.
The scheme is a central sector scheme, fully funded by the Union Government.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the PM-Vishwakarma Scheme in preserving traditional crafts and empowering informal sector workers.
Discuss the challenges faced by traditional artisans in India and how government schemes address them.
Evaluate the role of skill development and market linkages in fostering inclusive growth in the MSME sector.
Suggest measures to improve the outreach and effectiveness of welfare schemes for vulnerable communities.
GS2.imageDescription: An Indian artisan meticulously working on a traditional craft.
GS2.trivia: India's handicraft sector is one of the largest employment generators after agriculture, preserving diverse cultural heritage.

GS2.headline: India Continues to Champion Global South Agenda Post-G20 Presidency
GS2.explainer:
• India maintains its active role in advocating for the interests of the Global South, even after concluding its G20 presidency.
• The focus remains on key issues such as equitable climate finance, debt relief for developing nations, and technology transfer.
• India is actively engaging with various multilateral platforms and regional groupings to amplify the 'Voice of the Global South'.
• This ongoing diplomatic push aims to ensure that the concerns of developing countries are integrated into global decision-making.
• Initiatives like the 'Voice of Global South Summit' continue to serve as a platform for these discussions.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
GS2.source: Ministry of External Affairs, The Hindu
prelimsPoints: India held the G20 Presidency from December 1, 2022, to November 30, 2023.
The 'Voice of Global South Summit' was initiated by India.
The concept of 'Common But Differentiated Responsibilities' (CBDR) is central to climate finance discussions.
The African Union was admitted as a permanent member of the G20 during India's presidency.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's leadership role in representing the interests of the Global South on international platforms.
Discuss the key challenges faced by developing countries that India aims to address through its diplomatic efforts.
Evaluate the impact of India's G20 presidency on shaping global agendas and fostering South-South cooperation.
Examine the concept of 'climate justice' and its relevance in current international climate negotiations.
GS2.imageDescription: A world map highlighting countries of the Global South, symbolizing India's outreach.
GS2.trivia: The term 'Global South' broadly refers to countries in Asia, Africa, and Latin America, often characterized by shared experiences of colonialism and economic development challenges.

GS2.headline: India-EU Trade and Technology Council Focuses on Critical Minerals and Green Technologies
GS2.explainer:
• The second meeting of the India-EU Trade and Technology Council (TTC) saw significant discussions on critical minerals and green technologies.
• Both sides emphasized securing resilient supply chains for critical raw materials essential for the digital and green transitions.
• Cooperation on renewable energy, sustainable manufacturing, and circular economy principles was a key agenda item.
• The TTC aims to deepen strategic cooperation, facilitate trade, and address technological challenges between India and the European Union.
• This partnership is crucial for diversifying supply chains and strengthening strategic autonomy in a changing geopolitical landscape.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
GS2.source: Ministry of Commerce & Industry, European Commission
prelimsPoints: The India-EU Trade and Technology Council was established in 2022.
Critical minerals like Lithium, Cobalt, and Rare Earth Elements are vital for electric vehicles and renewable energy technologies.
The EU is one of India's largest trading partners.
The TTC is a strategic mechanism to manage trade, technology, and security issues.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of the India-EU Trade and Technology Council for both partners in the current geopolitical scenario.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities in securing critical mineral supply chains for India's energy transition.
Examine how cooperation in green technologies can foster sustainable development and economic growth.
Evaluate the potential of the TTC to diversify trade and reduce dependencies on single-source suppliers.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and the European Union displayed together, symbolizing cooperation.
GS2.trivia: The EU is India's third-largest trading partner, accounting for 10.8% of India's total trade in 2022-23.

GS2.headline: NEP 2020 Implementation Sees Progress in Higher Education Reforms
GS2.explainer:
• The implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 is showing discernible progress, particularly in reforming higher education.
• Key reforms include promoting a multidisciplinary approach, flexible curriculum, Academic Bank of Credits (ABC), and vocational education integration.
• Universities and colleges are gradually adopting the new credit system and offering multiple entry/exit options for students.
• The focus is on making higher education more holistic, accessible, and aligned with industry needs.
• Challenges persist in faculty recruitment, infrastructure development, and ensuring equitable implementation across diverse institutions.
gsSubTopics: Development processes and the development industry—the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 replaced the National Policy on Education of 1986.
The Academic Bank of Credits (ABC) allows students to store and transfer academic credits.
The policy aims to increase the Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in higher education to 50% by 2035.
Multidisciplinary approach means integrating various subjects like arts, science, commerce, and vocational studies.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of NEP 2020 for India's higher education system.
Discuss the key reforms introduced in higher education under NEP 2020 and their expected outcomes.
Examine the challenges in implementing NEP 2020, particularly concerning faculty, infrastructure, and institutional readiness.
Evaluate how the policy addresses the needs of India's demographic dividend and future workforce requirements.
GS2.imageDescription: Students engaging in a multidisciplinary learning environment at a university campus.
GS2.trivia: NEP 2020 is the first education policy of the 21st century in India.

GS2.headline: Smart Cities Mission Progress Reviewed, Focus on Sustainable Urban Development
GS2.explainer:
• A recent review assessed the progress of the Smart Cities Mission, highlighting achievements and areas for improvement.
• The mission focuses on integrated urban planning, digital infrastructure, and citizen-centric services to enhance livability.
• Key successes include improvements in urban mobility, waste management, and the establishment of Integrated Command and Control Centres (ICCCs).
• The review emphasized the need for greater capacity building for urban local bodies and ensuring financial sustainability of projects.
• Future efforts will prioritize climate-resilient urban infrastructure and equitable access to smart solutions.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
GS2.source: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
prelimsPoints: The Smart Cities Mission was launched in 2015.
It aims to promote cities that provide core infrastructure, a clean and sustainable environment, and a decent quality of life.
Integrated Command and Control Centres (ICCCs) are central to smart governance and monitoring.
Other related urban missions include AMRUT (Atal Mission for Rejuvenation and Urban Transformation) and HRIDAY (Heritage City Development and Augmentation Yojana).
mainsPoints: Analyze the objectives and achievements of the Smart Cities Mission in transforming India's urban landscape.
Discuss the challenges faced in implementing smart city projects, including funding, governance, and citizen participation.
Examine how the mission contributes to sustainable urban development and climate resilience.
Suggest measures to enhance the effectiveness and inclusivity of smart city initiatives for all urban residents.
GS2.imageDescription: A futuristic cityscape with smart infrastructure and green spaces, representing a smart city.
GS2.trivia: India is projected to have 600 million people living in urban areas by 2030, making urban planning crucial.

GS2.headline: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission Expands Reach, Focuses on Universal Health Coverage
GS2.explainer:
• The Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) is significantly expanding its reach to create a seamless online platform for health services.
• The mission aims to develop the backbone necessary to support the integrated digital health infrastructure of the country.
• Key components include the Ayushman Bharat Health Account (ABHA) number, healthcare professional registry, and health facility registry.
• The expansion focuses on enabling universal health coverage and improving access to healthcare services through digital means.
• Challenges include digital literacy, data privacy concerns, and integrating diverse healthcare providers.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
E-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential
GS2.source: National Health Authority (NHA), Ministry of Health & Family Welfare
prelimsPoints: ABDM was launched nationwide in September 2021.
The ABHA (Ayushman Bharat Health Account) number is a unique 14-digit health ID.
The National Health Authority (NHA) is the implementing agency for ABDM.
Ayushman Bharat also includes PM-JAY (Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana) for health insurance.
mainsPoints: Analyze the potential of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission to transform healthcare delivery and achieve universal health coverage in India.
Discuss the key features and benefits of creating an integrated digital health infrastructure.
Examine the challenges related to data privacy, digital literacy, and interoperability in implementing ABDM.
Evaluate how digital health initiatives can address disparities in healthcare access and quality across different regions.
GS2.imageDescription: A smartphone screen displaying the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission logo and health services.
GS2.trivia: India's digital health ecosystem aims to connect patients, doctors, and other healthcare stakeholders digitally.

GS3.headline: Gagangir Terror Attack: Seven Workers Killed in Jammu & Kashmir Tunnel Project
GS3.explainer:
• Seven workers involved in a critical tunnel project were killed in a militant attack on their camp in Gagangir, Ganderbal district of Jammu and Kashmir.
• The incident highlights the persistent security challenges in the region, particularly targeting development infrastructure and non-local workers.
• Security forces have launched a massive cordon and search operation to track down the perpetrators.
• The attack underscores the need for enhanced security measures for vital infrastructure projects in sensitive areas.
• Political leaders and civil society have condemned the attack, reiterating calls for peace and stability.
gsSubTopics: Linkages between development and spread of extremism
Role of external state and non-state actors in creating challenges to internal security
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Various security forces and agencies and their mandate
prelimsPoints: Gagangir is located in the Ganderbal district of Jammu and Kashmir.
The attack targeted workers on a key infrastructure project.
Security challenges in J&K often involve external state and non-state actors.
mainsPoints: Analyze the impact of such attacks on development initiatives and investor confidence in J&K.
Discuss the effectiveness of current counter-terrorism strategies in addressing persistent militant threats.
Examine the role of local population and intelligence gathering in preventing such incidents.
Evaluate the challenges in securing critical infrastructure projects in conflict-prone regions.
GS3.imageDescription: Security forces conducting search operations in the mountainous terrain of Gagangir, J&K.
GS3.trivia: The Zojila Tunnel, a strategically important project in J&K, aims to provide all-weather connectivity between Srinagar, Kargil, and Leh.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Internal Security
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Linkages between development and spread of extremism

GS3.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Strict Interpretation of Forest Conservation Act, Emphasizes Deemed Forests
GS3.explainer:
• The Supreme Court delivered a landmark ruling, reinforcing the strict application of the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980, especially concerning 'deemed forests'.
• The judgment clarifies that areas recorded as forests in government records, irrespective of their legal classification, fall under the Act's purview.
• This decision significantly impacts infrastructure projects and development activities requiring forest land diversion, mandating prior central government approval.
• It also has implications for tribal rights and the implementation of the Forest Rights Act, 2006, by protecting traditional forest-dwelling communities.
• The Court directed states to expedite the identification and demarcation of 'deemed forests' as per the T.N. Godavarman Thirumulpad case.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
prelimsPoints: The Forest (Conservation) Act was enacted in 1980.
The concept of 'deemed forests' was introduced by the Supreme Court in the T.N. Godavarman Thirumulpad case (1996).
Prior central government approval is mandatory for diverting forest land for non-forest purposes.
The Forest Rights Act, 2006, grants rights to forest-dwelling Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers.
mainsPoints: Analyze the implications of the Supreme Court's ruling on the balance between development and environmental conservation.
Discuss the challenges in identifying and demarcating 'deemed forests' across different states.
Examine how this judgment strengthens the protection of biodiversity and the rights of indigenous communities.
Evaluate the role of the judiciary in environmental governance and policy implementation in India.
GS3.imageDescription: Lush green forest canopy, symbolizing forest conservation efforts.
GS3.trivia: The T.N. Godavarman Thirumulpad case is often referred to as the 'forest case' and has significantly shaped forest jurisprudence in India.

GS3.headline: India Champions Global Adoption of Digital Public Infrastructure Model
GS3.explainer:
• India is actively promoting its successful Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) model, comprising Aadhaar, UPI, and ONDC, for global adoption, especially in the Global South.
• The initiative aims to foster financial inclusion, streamline public services, and boost digital economies in developing nations.
• Discussions are ongoing at various international forums, including the G20, on interoperability, data governance, and technology transfer.
• India's DPI model offers a cost-effective and scalable solution for digital transformation, reducing reliance on proprietary systems.
• The push highlights India's growing influence in digital diplomacy and its commitment to shared prosperity.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
prelimsPoints: Aadhaar is India's unique identification system.
UPI (Unified Payments Interface) is a real-time payment system.
ONDC (Open Network for Digital Commerce) is an initiative to democratize e-commerce.
India's DPI model is often referred to as the 'India Stack'.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key components of India's Digital Public Infrastructure and their impact on socio-economic development.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities for global adoption of the DPI model, particularly in developing countries.
Examine how India's digital diplomacy, through DPI, can enhance its leadership in the Global South.
Evaluate the potential risks associated with data privacy and security in a widely adopted DPI framework.
GS3.imageDescription: Infographic showing the interconnectedness of Aadhaar, UPI, and ONDC as part of India's DPI.
GS3.trivia: UPI transactions surpassed 10 billion per month in 2023, showcasing its widespread adoption.

GS3.headline: ISRO Provides Update on Shukrayaan-1, India's Upcoming Venus Orbiter Mission
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) has provided an update on the progress of Shukrayaan-1, India's ambitious mission to Venus.
• The mission aims to study Venus's atmospheric chemistry, surface composition, and subsurface structure using advanced scientific instruments.
• Shukrayaan-1 is slated for launch by late 2024 or early 2025, marking India's first dedicated mission to Venus.
• The mission involves collaboration with international partners for some payloads, enhancing global scientific cooperation.
• This venture underscores India's growing capabilities in planetary exploration and its contribution to space science.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Awareness in the fields of Space
prelimsPoints: Shukrayaan-1 is India's proposed mission to Venus.
Venus is often called Earth's 'sister planet' due to similar size and mass.
ISRO has previously launched successful missions to Mars (Mangalyaan) and the Moon (Chandrayaan series).
The mission will carry a Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) to map Venus's surface.
mainsPoints: Discuss the scientific objectives and potential discoveries of the Shukrayaan-1 mission.
Analyze the significance of India's planetary exploration program for its scientific and technological advancement.
Examine the role of international collaboration in complex space missions like Shukrayaan-1.
Evaluate the broader socio-economic benefits derived from advancements in space technology.
GS3.imageDescription: Artistic rendition of the Shukrayaan-1 orbiter orbiting Venus.
GS3.trivia: Venus is the hottest planet in our solar system, with surface temperatures hot enough to melt lead, despite being farther from the Sun than Mercury.

GS3.headline: National Green Hydrogen Mission Drives Investment in Renewable Energy Sector
GS3.explainer:
• India's National Green Hydrogen Mission is successfully catalyzing significant domestic and international investments in the renewable energy sector.
• The mission focuses on developing green hydrogen production capacity and promoting its use in hard-to-abate sectors like fertilizers, refining, and steel.
• Incentives under the Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition (SIGHT) Programme are boosting electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production.
• This initiative is crucial for India to achieve its decarbonization targets, enhance energy security, and become a global hub for green hydrogen.
• Challenges include the high initial cost of green hydrogen production and the need for robust infrastructure development.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life; Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
GS3.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy, Business Standard
prelimsPoints: Green hydrogen is produced by splitting water using renewable electricity (electrolysis).
The National Green Hydrogen Mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export.
The SIGHT Programme provides financial incentives for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production.
India has set a target to achieve Net-Zero emissions by 2070.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of the National Green Hydrogen Mission for India's energy security and climate goals.
Discuss the economic potential of green hydrogen for various industries and its role in decarbonization.
Examine the challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production and adoption in India.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government incentives like the SIGHT Programme in driving investment and innovation.
GS3.imageDescription: A conceptual image showing a green hydrogen production plant with wind turbines and solar panels.
GS3.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, but it rarely exists freely on Earth.

GS3.headline: Private Sector Participation Bolsters India's Defence Indigenization Efforts
GS3.explainer:
